1 # Portfolio Scorecard — Requirements 2 ## Archetype Section Updates 3 4 **Source:** Post-meeting notes · March 16, 2026 5 **File:** `portfolio-scorecard.html` 6 **Section:** `00 — Context` → Designer archetype area 7 8 --- 9 10 ## REQ-01: Add Explainer Text to Section Header 11 12 **Status:** Ready to build 13 **Priority:** — 14 **Section affected:** Section 00 (Context) → Designer archetype subsection 15 16 ### Current state 17 18 The archetype area has a single label: 19 20 > **Designer archetype** *(optional)* 21 22 No additional context is provided to help the user understand what archetype selection means or how to choose one. 23 24 ### Requirement 25 26 Add subtitle/helper copy beneath the "Designer archetype" label that explains the purpose of the selection. The explainer should communicate two ideas: 27 28 1. What part of design do you enjoy the most? 29 2. What are you best at? 30 31 This is **header-level subtitle text** — the same pattern used on the section header's `section-subtitle` class — not per-pill descriptions or tooltips. 32 33 ### Reference (current pattern in codebase) 34 35 ```html 36 37
38 Who are you, and what type of designer owns this portfolio? 39
40 ``` 41 42 The archetype explainer should follow this same visual hierarchy, sitting between the "Designer archetype" label and the pill row. 43 44 ### Suggested copy (refine as needed) 45 46 > Select the archetype that best describes this designer — what part of design they enjoy most, or what they're best at. 47 48 --- 49 50 ## REQ-02: Custom Archetype Input 51 52 **Status:** Ready to build 53 **Priority:** — 54 **Section affected:** Section 00 (Context) → Designer archetype area (end of pill row) 55 56 ### Current state 57 58 The user can select from 7 predefined archetypes (Storyteller, Systems Architect, Brand Worldbuilder, Interaction Crafter, Service Designer, Research Strategist, Creative Technologist). If none fits, there is no alternative — the field is simply left empty. 59 60 ### Requirement 61 62 Add a custom input option **after** the existing archetype pills that allows the user to type their own archetype name if none of the 7 presets apply. 63 64 **Behavior:** 65 66 - Appears at the end of the archetype pill row (or immediately below it) 67 - Selecting a preset archetype should clear the custom input, and vice versa — they are mutually exclusive 68 - The custom archetype name should flow into the results panel wherever `designerName`'s archetype is displayed 69 - Custom input should be styled consistently with the existing pill/input aesthetic 70 71 ### Open questions 72 73 | # | Question | Notes | 74 |---|----------|-------| 75 | 1 | Should a custom archetype include guidance fields (strength to show, watch for, case study emphasis)? | If yes, the results panel would need 3 additional text inputs. If no, the guidance section simply won't appear for custom archetypes. | 76 | 2 | Should the section label change from "Designer archetype" to something friendlier? | Options: replace the label entirely with the new framing, or keep "Designer archetype" and add the explainer as helper text below it. | 77 78 ### Engine impact 79 80 - `ARCHETYPES` array: No change to existing entries. Custom archetype would be stored as a separate state value (e.g., `customArchetype: string`). 81 - `PORTFOLIO_GUIDANCE` map: Would not have an entry for custom archetypes unless REQ-02 open question #1 is resolved as "yes." 82 - Results panel: Needs a conditional — if custom archetype is set, display the name but skip or adapt the guidance section. 83 84 ### Suggested state changes 85 86 ```javascript 87 // New state 88 const [customArchetype, setCustomArchetype] = useState(''); 89 90 // Derived 91 const activeArchetype = archetype || (customArchetype ? 'custom' : null); 92 const activeArchetypeName = archetype 93 ? ARCHETYPES.find(a => a.id === archetype)?.name 94 : customArchetype || null; 95 ``` 96 97 --- 98 99 ## REQ-03: Add Career Stage Section 100 101 **Status:** Ready to build 102 **Priority:** — 103 **Section affected:** New section in the input form (before current Section 01 — Portfolio Shell) 104 **Source:** Existing implementation in `ux-designer-scorecard.html`, Section 01 105 106 ### Requirement 107 108 Port the Career Stage selection from the UX Designer Scorecard into the Portfolio Scorecard. This is a single-select row of career level buttons, each displaying a title and a year-range badge. 109 110 ### Data model (from `ux-designer-scorecard.html`) 111 112 ```javascript 113 const CAREER_LEVELS = [ 114 { id: 'student', label: 'Student / Intern', yrs: '0 yrs' }, 115 { id: 'junior', label: 'Junior', yrs: '0–2 yrs' }, 116 { id: 'mid', label: 'Mid-Level', yrs: '2–5 yrs' }, 117 { id: 'senior', label: 'Senior', yrs: '5–8 yrs' }, 118 { id: 'lead', label: 'Lead', yrs: '7–10 yrs' }, 119 { id: 'principal', label: 'Principal / Staff', yrs: '10+ yrs' }, 120 { id: 'manager', label: 'Manager / Director', yrs: '8+ yrs' }, 121 { id: 'vp', label: 'VP / Head of Design', yrs: '12+ yrs' }, 122 ]; 123 ``` 124 125 ### UI pattern (from `ux-designer-scorecard.html`) 126 127 - Horizontal row of pill-style buttons (`.ladder` / `.ladder-btn` classes) 128 - Each button shows the level label and a monospace year badge below it 129 - Single-select toggle: clicking the active level deselects it 130 - Selected state uses coral accent color in the designer scorecard; should adapt to the portfolio scorecard's teal palette or follow whatever color the Context section uses 131 132 ### Placement 133 134 Currently the Portfolio Scorecard's Section 00 (Context) contains review type and designer archetype. Career Stage should be added as a new subsection within Section 00, or promoted to its own numbered section. This shifts the existing section numbers: 135 136 | Current | Proposed | 137 |---------|----------| 138 | 00 — Context (review type + archetype) | 00 — Context (review type + archetype + career stage) | 139 | 01 — Portfolio Shell | 01 — Portfolio Shell | 140 | 02 — Case Studies | 02 — Case Studies | 141 | 03 — Portfolio Strategy | 03 — Portfolio Strategy | 142 143 Alternatively, if Career Stage gets its own section number, all subsequent sections shift by one. 144 145 ### State changes 146 147 ```javascript 148 // New state 149 const [careerLevel, setCareerLevel] = useState(null); 150 151 // Add to reset handler 152 setCareerLevel(null); 153 154 // Pass to results panel 155 const resultsProps = { ..., careerLevel }; 156 ``` 157 158 ### Results panel impact 159 160 The UX Designer Scorecard displays the selected career level as a metadata field in the results sidebar. The same pattern should apply here — show the selected career stage as a contextual label in the Portfolio Assessment results panel. 161 162 --- 163 164 ## REQ-04: Company Career Ladder Toggle (Home Depot Blueprint) < truncated lines 165-1279 > 1280 1281 **Engine/code references (internal — less visible but keep consistent):** 1282 1283 Variable names like `caseStudies`, `CS_DIMS`, `csScores`, etc. can stay unchanged to avoid a large refactor — these are internal and don't affect what users see. The rename is a skin/copy concern, not an engine restructure. 1284 1285 --- 1286 1287 ## REQ-13: Portfolio Purpose Framework & Leadership Dimensions 1288 1289 **Status:** Brainstorming — needs exploration before build 1290 **Priority:** — 1291 **Type:** Strategic / structural — may reshape existing dimensions or become a separate tool 1292 1293 > **⚠️ Implementation note:** This REQ should always remain the last item in the backlog. It is an exploratory / "play" layer that builds on top of the scorecard. Any time the base scorecard changes (new dimensions, restructured sections, scoring changes, etc.), a **scorecard version bump** is required before this REQ can be revisited. This ensures the foundation is stable before layering analytical frameworks on top of it. 1294 1295 ### Raw meeting notes 1296 1297 **Leadership skills in portfolios:** 1298 - Being personable — storytelling, branding, awareness of communication, distilling complex projects into simplicity 1299 - "The work you do speaks for you" 1300 1301 **What portfolios are for (three layers):** 1302 1303 1. **Communicate** — what you know, and prove you're good at it 1304 2. **Communicate** — what you do, who you are, what you're about, what projects you've worked on — and you want to be wowed 1305 3. **Convey** — how you work, how you problem solve 1306 1307 **Key assertion:** It should not be cookie cutter. These are all measurable things. 1308 1309 --- 1310 1311 ### Analysis: What's being described 1312 1313 These notes are articulating a *philosophy of portfolio evaluation* — the "why" behind the dimensions we're already scoring. They break down into three distinct evaluation lenses: 1314 1315 | Lens | Question it answers | What it measures | 1316 |------|-------------------|------------------| 1317 | **Competence** | "Are they good?" | Knowledge, skill depth, craft quality | 1318 | **Identity** | "Who are they?" | Personality, brand, positioning, wow factor | 1319 | **Process** | "How do they think?" | Problem-solving, methodology, leadership in complexity | 1320 1321 The current scorecard *partially* covers these across its three sections, but the mapping is implicit rather than explicit: 1322 1323 | Current dimension | Lens it serves | 1324 |-------------------|---------------| 1325 | First Impression | Identity | 1326 | About & Personal Brand | Identity | 1327 | Storytelling (REQ-11) | Identity + Process | 1328 | Visual Design Quality | Competence | 1329 | Communication: Writing & Copy (REQ-11) | Competence + Identity | 1330 | Problem Framing (case study) | Process | 1331 | Process & Methodology (case study) | Process | 1332 | Outcomes & Impact (case study) | Competence | 1333 | Differentiation (strategy) | Identity | 1334 1335 **The leadership angle** — being personable, distilling complexity, work speaking for itself — is not currently captured as a standalone dimension anywhere. It's a meta-skill: can this designer *lead through their portfolio*? Can they make you trust their judgment before you've ever met them? 1336 1337 --- 1338 1339 ### Three paths forward 1340 1341 **Path A: Integrate into the existing scorecard** 1342 1343 Add these as new dimensions or rename existing ones to make the three lenses explicit. No new tool required. 1344 1345 Where it could fit without structural changes: 1346 1347 | Concept | Proposed integration | 1348 |---------|---------------------| 1349 | "Prove you're good at it" | Already covered by Outcomes & Impact, Visual Execution | 1350 | "Who you are / what you're about" | Already covered by About & Personal Brand | 1351 | "Want to be wowed" | Already covered by First Impression, Differentiation | 1352 | "How you problem solve" | Already covered by Problem Framing, Process & Methodology | 1353 | "Not cookie cutter" | Already covered by Differentiation | 1354 | "Distilling complexity" | **Gap** — could add as a new shell or strategy dimension: "Clarity of Communication" or fold into Communication: Writing & Copy | 1355 | "Personable / leadership presence" | **Gap** — could add as a new shell dimension: "Leadership Presence" or "Designer Voice" | 1356 | "Work speaks for you" | **Gap** — this is a meta-quality about self-evidence. Could become a strategy dimension: "Self-Evidence" — does the portfolio prove competence without needing to be explained? | 1357 1358 **Pros:** Low effort, keeps everything in one tool. 1359 **Cons:** The scorecard is already growing (9 shell dims + Storytelling). Adding more risks dimension fatigue. 1360 1361 **Path B: Separate "Portfolio Philosophy" evaluation tool** 1362 1363 A standalone companion document or tool that asks higher-level questions. Less granular than the scorecard — more like a rubric or checklist that a reviewer fills out in prose. Think of it as the *qualitative* counterpart to the scorecard's *quantitative* ratings. 1364 1365 Structure could follow the three lenses directly: 1366 1367 ``` 1368 Section 1: Competence — Does this portfolio prove they're good? 1369 Section 2: Identity — Do you know who this designer is? 1370 Section 3: Process — Do you understand how they think? 1371 Section 4: Leadership — Would you trust this person to lead? 1372 ``` 1373 1374 Each section has 2–3 guided prompts with open text responses rather than 1–5 dot ratings. 1375 1376 **Pros:** Clean separation of concerns. Quantitative scores stay in the scorecard, qualitative evaluation lives here. Can be used independently or alongside the scorecard. 1377 **Cons:** Another tool to maintain. Users might not want to fill out both. 1378 1379 **Path C: Add a "Portfolio Philosophy" overlay to the existing scorecard** 1380 1381 The three lenses become a *secondary grouping* on the results panel. The dimensions stay the same, but the results view can toggle between "by section" (Shell / Case Studies / Strategy) and "by lens" (Competence / Identity / Process). This reframes the same scores through a different analytical angle without adding any new input fields. 1382 1383 ```javascript 1384 const LENSES = { 1385 competence: { 1386 label: 'Competence', 1387 desc: 'Does this portfolio prove they are good at what they do?', 1388 dimensions: ['visual_quality', 'outcomes', 'visual_execution', 'writing_quality'], 1389 }, 1390 identity: { 1391 label: 'Identity', 1392 desc: 'Do you know who this designer is and what they stand for?', 1393 dimensions: ['first_impression', 'about_story', 'storytelling', 'differentiation', 'contact_cta'], 1394 }, 1395 process: { 1396 label: 'Process', 1397 desc: 'Do you understand how they think and solve problems?', 1398 dimensions: ['problem_framing', 'process_depth', 'role_clarity', 'navigation'], 1399 }, 1400 }; 1401 ``` 1402 1403 **Pros:** Zero new input fields. Reuses existing data. Adds analytical depth to the results panel. 1404 **Cons:** Some dimensions don't map cleanly to one lens. Could feel forced. 1405 1406 --- 1407 1408 ### Recommendation 1409 1410 **Start with Path A (small) + Path C (results view).** Add 1–2 missing dimensions to cover the gaps (leadership presence, self-evidence), then add the lens-based results view as an alternative analysis mode. This delivers the most value with the least structural change. If the concept proves useful in workshops, Path B (standalone tool) becomes a follow-up project. 1411 1412 ### Open questions 1413 1414 | # | Question | Notes | 1415 |---|----------|-------| 1416 | 1 | Is this for the portfolio scorecard, a separate tool, or both? | Path A, B, or C above | 1417 | 2 | Should "leadership presence" be a shell dimension, a strategy dimension, or something else? | Shell = evaluating the site's tone. Strategy = evaluating positioning choices. | 1418 | 3 | How important is the "not cookie cutter" signal? | Differentiation partially covers this, but it might warrant stronger language or a more prominent placement. | 1419 | 4 | Should the three lenses (Competence / Identity / Process) be visible to the user filling out the scorecard, or only in the results analysis? | Showing them during input could guide better ratings. Showing only in results keeps the input simple. | 1420 1421 --- 1422 1423 ## Summary 1424 1425 | ID | Requirement | Status | Open Questions | 1426 |----|------------|--------|----------------| 1427 | REQ-01 | Explainer subtitle on archetype section header | Ready to build | None | 1428 | REQ-02 | Custom archetype text input | Ready to build | 2 (guidance fields, label copy) | 1429 | REQ-03 | Add Career Stage section (ported from UX Designer Scorecard) | Ready to build | Placement (subsection of 00 vs. own section) | 1430 | REQ-04 | Company career ladder toggle (Home Depot blueprint) | Ready to build | 3 (extensibility, visual detail, attribution) | 1431 | REQ-05 | Career Goal / Next Step section | Ready to build | None (copy refinement is a skin concern) | 1432 | REQ-06 | Per-dimension notes (all rated areas) | Ready to build | None | 1433 | REQ-07 | Design Disciplines (multi-select + custom input) | Ready to build | None | 1434 | REQ-08 | Rebrand footer to "Jax UX" logo lockup | Ready to build | None | 1435 | REQ-09 | Replace Print with Download (client-side export) | Ready to build | 2 (PNG vs PDF, keep print styles) | 1436 | REQ-10 | Shareable state via URL + QR code | Ready to build | None | 1437 | REQ-11 | Shell updates: rename Writing & Copy, add Storytelling dim | Ready to build | None | 1438 | REQ-12 | General Feedback section (bottom of form) | Ready to build | None | 1439 | REQ-14 | AI Tools & Fluency (Adaptability) | Ready to build | 1 (broader adaptability scope — what else belongs here?) | 1440 | REQ-15 | Accessibility pass & typography scale | Ready to build (Phase 1 type scale, Phase 2 full WCAG audit) | None | 1441 | REQ-16 | Rename "Case Studies" → "Project / Case Study" | Ready to build | None | 1442 | REQ-13 | Portfolio Purpose Framework & Leadership dims | Brainstorming (always last — requires scorecard version bump) | 4 (scope, placement, lens visibility, standalone vs integrated) | 1443

This reframes the same scores through a different analytical angle without adding any new input fields. ```javascript const LENSES = { competence: { label: 'Competence', desc: 'Does this portfolio prove they are good at what they do?', dimensions: ['visual_quality', 'outcomes', 'visual_execution', 'writing_quality'], }, identity: { label: 'Identity', desc: 'Do you know who this designer is and what they stand for?', dimensions: ['first_impression', 'about_story', 'storytelling', 'differentiation', 'contact_cta'], }, process: { label: 'Process', desc: 'Do you understand how they think and solve problems?', dimensions: ['problem_framing', 'process_depth', 'role_clarity', 'navigation'], }, }; ``` **Pros:** Zero new input fields. Reuses existing data. Adds analytical depth to the results panel. **Cons:** Some dimensions don't map cleanly to one lens. Could feel forced. --- ### Recommendation **Start with Path A (small) + Path C (results view).** Add 1–2 missing dimensions to cover the gaps (leadership presence, self-evidence), then add the lens-based results view as an alternative analysis mode. This delivers the most value with the least structural change. If the concept proves useful in workshops, Path B (standalone tool) becomes a follow-up project. ### Open questions | # | Question | Notes | |---|----------|-------| | 1 | Is this for the portfolio scorecard, a separate tool, or both? | Path A, B, or C above | | 2 | Should "leadership presence" be a shell dimension, a strategy dimension, or something else? | Shell = evaluating the site's tone. Strategy = evaluating positioning choices. | | 3 | How important is the "not cookie cutter" signal? | Differentiation partially covers this, but it might warrant stronger language or a more prominent placement. | | 4 | Should the three lenses (Competence / Identity / Process) be visible to the user filling out the scorecard, or only in the results analysis? | Showing them during input could guide better ratings. Showing only in results keeps the input simple. | --- ## Summary | ID | Requirement | Status | Open Questions | |----|------------|--------|----------------| | REQ-01 | Explainer subtitle on archetype section header | Ready to build | None | | REQ-02 | Custom archetype text input | Ready to build | 2 (guidance fields, label copy) | | REQ-03 | Add Career Stage section (ported from UX Designer Scorecard) | Ready to build | Placement (subsection of 00 vs. own section) | | REQ-04 | Company career ladder toggle (Home Depot blueprint) | Ready to build | 3 (extensibility, visual detail, attribution) | | REQ-05 | Career Goal / Next Step section | Ready to build | None (copy refinement is a skin concern) | | REQ-06 | Per-dimension notes (all rated areas) | Ready to build | None | | REQ-07 | Design Disciplines (multi-select + custom input) | Ready to build | None | | REQ-08 | Rebrand footer to "Jax UX" logo lockup | Ready to build | None | | REQ-09 | Replace Print with Download (client-side export) | Ready to build | 2 (PNG vs PDF, keep print styles) | | REQ-10 | Shareable state via URL + QR code | Ready to build | None | | REQ-11 | Shell updates: rename Writing & Copy, add Storytelling dim | Ready to build | None | | REQ-12 | General Feedback section (bottom of form) | Ready to build | None | | REQ-14 | AI Tools & Fluency (Adaptability) | Ready to build | 1 (broader adaptability scope — what else belongs here?) | | REQ-15 | Accessibility pass & typography scale | Ready to build (Phase 1 type scale, Phase 2 full WCAG audit) | None | | REQ-16 | Rename "Case Studies" → "Project / Case Study" | Ready to build | None | | REQ-13 | Portfolio Purpose Framework & Leadership dims | Brainstorming (always last — requires scorecard version bump) | 4 (scope, placement, lens visibility, standalone vs integrated) | ", "filename": "/mnt/project/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md"}

1	# The Engine — Data Models, Algorithms & Logic
     2	## Extracted from UX Designer Scorecard + Competency Radar
     3	
     4	Everything in this document is **what the system knows and how it thinks**. Nothing about colors, fonts, layouts, or animations. You could render this engine as a spreadsheet, a CLI tool, or a voice interface and it would still work.
     5	
     6	---
     7	
     8	## 1. Two Separate Frameworks (The Core Tension)
     9	
    10	The Scorecard and the Radar currently run on **completely different competency models**. This is the most important thing to understand about the engine as it stands today.
    11	
    12	### Framework A: Craft Skills (Scorecard)
    13	Measures **what a designer can do** — their hands-on discipline expertise.
    14	
    15	| ID | Skill | Description |
    16	|----|-------|-------------|
    17	| `research` | User Research | Interviews, usability testing, synthesis, insight generation |
    18	| `ia` | Information Architecture | Content structure, navigation, taxonomy, sitemaps |
    19	| `interaction` | Interaction Design | Flows, wireframes, prototyping, micro-interactions |
    20	| `visual` | Visual Design | Layout, typography, color, iconography, brand expression |
    21	| `systems` | Design Systems | Components, tokens, documentation, governance |
    22	| `accessibility` | Accessibility | WCAG, assistive tech, inclusive patterns |
    23	| `strategy` | Product Strategy | Roadmapping, metrics, stakeholder alignment, OKRs |
    24	| `leadership` | Design Leadership | Mentoring, critique, team building, org influence |
    25	| `ops` | Design Operations | Process, tooling, team scaling, workflow management |
    26	| `craft` | Prototyping & Craft | Hi-fi execution, motion, code awareness, tool mastery |
    27	
    28	**Scale:** 1–5 (Novice → Expert). User self-rates. Max total: 50.
    29	
    30	### Framework B: Leadership Competencies (Radar)
    31	Measures **how a designer operates** — their behavioral and strategic profile. Adapted from the Korn Ferry Leadership Architect framework.
    32	
    33	| Index | Competency | Description |
    34	|-------|-----------|-------------|
    35	| 0 | Strategic Thinking | Seeing the big picture, anticipating future needs |
    36	| 1 | Business Acumen | Understanding organizational context and value |
    37	| 2 | Stakeholder Influence | Persuading, aligning, building coalition |
    38	| 3 | Craft Mastery | Depth of design skill and judgment |
    39	| 4 | User Empathy | Understanding people deeply |
    40	| 5 | Communication & Story | Translating ideas across audiences |
    41	| 6 | Team Development | Growing others, building culture |
    42	| 7 | Systems Thinking | Connecting parts to wholes |
    43	| 8 | Innovation | Pushing boundaries, tolerating ambiguity |
    44	| 9 | Execution & Delivery | Shipping, quality, reliability |
    45	
    46	**Scale:** 1–10 (in benchmark data). Not currently user-rated — these are reference profiles only.
    47	
    48	### The Gap
    49	There is no mapping between these two frameworks. A user rates themselves on Framework A, gets an archetype from Framework A's model, but then looks at Framework B's radar chart which uses a completely different lens. The two tools don't share data.
    50	
    51	---
    52	
    53	## 2. Archetype Models (Also Divergent)
    54	
    55	### Scorecard Archetypes (6)
    56	
    57	These are detected from the user's **craft skill self-ratings**:
    58	
    59	```javascript
    60	const ARCHETYPES = [
    61	  {
    62	    name: "The Researcher",
    63	    focus: "Deep in discovery — insight & evidence",
    64	    peaks: ["research", "strategy", "accessibility"]
    65	  },
    66	  {
    67	    name: "The Craftsperson",
    68	    focus: "Pixel-perfect polish, motion & detail",
    69	    peaks: ["visual", "craft", "interaction"]
    70	  },
    71	  {
    72	    name: "The Architect",
    73	    focus: "Systems thinker — scalable foundations",
    74	    peaks: ["systems", "ia", "ops"]
    75	  },
    76	  {
    77	    name: "The Strategist",
    78	    focus: "Big-picture, design meets business",
    79	    peaks: ["strategy", "leadership", "research"]
    80	  },
    81	  {
    82	    name: "The Generalist",
    83	    focus: "Adaptable, cross-functional, fills gaps",
    84	    peaks: []  // No peaks — detected by low variance
    85	  },
    86	  {
    87	    name: "The Champion",
    88	    focus: "Evangelist — teams, culture, buy-in",
    89	    peaks: ["leadership", "ops", "strategy"]
    90	  },
    91	];
    92	```
    93	
    94	### Radar Archetypes (7)
    95	
    96	These are predefined profiles with **no detection algorithm** — the user manually selects one:
    97	
    98	```javascript
    99	const ARCHETYPES = {
   100	  storyteller:  { name: "Experience Storyteller",  sub: "Disney · Pixar · Immersive" },
   101	  systems:      { name: "Systems Architect",       sub: "Enterprise · Fintech · Data" },
   102	  brand:        { name: "Brand Worldbuilder",      sub: "Agencies · Startups · Luxury" },
   103	  interaction:  { name: "Interaction Crafter",     sub: "Product · Design Systems" },
   104	  service:      { name: "Service Designer",        sub: "Gov · Healthcare · Consulting" },
   105	  strategist:   { name: "Research Strategist",     sub: "R&D · Innovation · Policy" },
   106	  technologist: { name: "Creative Technologist",   sub: "Labs · Installations · Experimental" },
   107	};
   108	```
   109	
   110	### The Mismatch
   111	| Scorecard | Radar | Notes |
   112	|-----------|-------|-------|
   113	| The Researcher | Research Strategist | Closest match |
   114	| The Craftsperson | Interaction Crafter | Partial overlap |
   115	| The Architect | Systems Architect | Close match |
   116	| The Strategist | (no direct match) | Radar has no pure "business strategist" |
   117	| The Generalist | (no equivalent) | Radar doesn't detect blends |
   118	| The Champion | (no equivalent) | Radar has no "evangelist" type |
   119	| (no equivalent) | Experience Storyteller | Scorecard has no narrative/emotion type |
   120	| (no equivalent) | Brand Worldbuilder | Scorecard has no brand identity type |
   121	| (no equivalent) | Service Designer | Scorecard has no service design type |
   122	| (no equivalent) | Creative Technologist | Scorecard has no code-craft hybrid |
   123	
   124	---
   125	
   126	## 3. Career Level Models (Also Different)
   127	
   128	### Scorecard Career Levels (8)
   129	
   130	```javascript
   131	const CAREER_LEVELS = [
   132	  { id: "student",   label: "Student / Intern",     yrs: "0 yrs" },
   133	  { id: "junior",    label: "Junior",               yrs: "0–2 yrs" },
   134	  { id: "mid",       label: "Mid-Level",            yrs: "2–5 yrs" },
   135	  { id: "senior",    label: "Senior",               yrs: "5–8 yrs" },
   136	  { id: "lead",      label: "Lead",                 yrs: "7–10 yrs" },
   137	  { id: "principal", label: "Principal / Staff",     yrs: "10+ yrs" },
   138	  { id: "manager",   label: "Manager / Director",   yrs: "8+ yrs" },
   139	  { id: "vp",        label: "VP / Head of Design",  yrs: "12+ yrs" },
   140	];
   141	```
   142	
   143	### Radar Career Levels (5)
   144	
   145	```javascript
   146	const LEVELS = {
   147	  junior:    "Designer I–II",
   148	  senior:    "Senior Designer",
   149	  staff:     "Staff Designer",
   150	  principal: "Principal / Director",
   151	  fellow:    "Fellow / Sr. Director",
   152	};
   153	```
   154	
   155	### The Mismatch
   156	The Scorecard has finer granularity (student, junior, mid, senior, lead, principal, manager, VP) while the Radar collapses these into broader bands. The Scorecard mixes IC and management into one list; the Radar is IC-only (no explicit management track). There is no mapping between the two.
   157	
   158	---
   159	
   160	## 4. The Benchmark Data Matrix (Radar Only)
   161	
   162	350 values: 7 archetypes × 5 levels × 10 competencies. This is the heart of the Radar — each combination produces a unique shape.
   163	
   164	```javascript

   169	    senior:    [4,    3,   4,    8,    8,    7,    3,    3,    6,    7],

   437	
   438	The chart remembers its previous state (`prevDataRef`) and morphs from old shape to new shape when selections change.
   439	
   440	### Sidebar Radar Geometry (Scorecard — simpler)
   441	
   442	```javascript
   443	const c = size / 2;             // center
   444	const r = size / 2 - 28;        // radius with label margin
   445	const levels = 5;               // ring count (matching 1–5 scale)
   446	const step = (Math.PI * 2) / ids.length;
   447	
   448	// Grid rings: polygonal (not circular)
   449	const rings = Array.from({ length: levels }, (_, i) => {

   453	    return `${c + rr * Math.cos(a)},${c + rr * Math.sin(a)}`;

{"language": "markdown", "code": "# The Engine — Data Models, Algorithms & Logic
## Extracted from UX Designer Scorecard + Competency Radar

Everything in this document is **what the system knows and how it thinks**. Nothing about colors, fonts, layouts, or animations. You could render this engine as a spreadsheet, a CLI tool, or a voice interface and it would still work.

---

## 1. Two Separate Frameworks (The Core Tension)

The Scorecard and the Radar currently run on **completely different competency models**. This is the most important thing to understand about the engine as it stands today.

### Framework A: Craft Skills (Scorecard)
Measures **what a designer can do** — their hands-on discipline expertise.

| ID | Skill | Description |
|----|-------|-------------|
| `research` | User Research | Interviews, usability testing, synthesis, insight generation |
| `ia` | Information Architecture | Content structure, navigation, taxonomy, sitemaps |
| `interaction` | Interaction Design | Flows, wireframes, prototyping, micro-interactions |
| `visual` | Visual Design | Layout, typography, color, iconography, brand expression |
| `systems` | Design Systems | Components, tokens, documentation, governance |
| `accessibility` | Accessibility | WCAG, assistive tech, inclusive patterns |
| `strategy` | Product Strategy | Roadmapping, metrics, stakeholder alignment, OKRs |
| `leadership` | Design Leadership | Mentoring, critique, team building, org influence |
| `ops` | Design Operations | Process, tooling, team scaling, workflow management |
| `craft` | Prototyping & Craft | Hi-fi execution, motion, code awareness, tool mastery |

**Scale:** 1–5 (Novice → Expert). User self-rates. Max total: 50.

### Framework B: Leadership Competencies (Radar)
Measures **how a designer operates** — their behavioral and strategic profile. Adapted from the Korn Ferry Leadership Architect framework.

| Index | Competency | Description |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | Strategic Thinking | Seeing the big picture, anticipating future needs |
| 1 | Business Acumen | Understanding organizational context and value |
| 2 | Stakeholder Influence | Persuading, aligning, building coalition |
| 3 | Craft Mastery | Depth of design skill and judgment |
| 4 | User Empathy | Understanding people deeply |
| 5 | Communication & Story | Translating ideas across audiences |
| 6 | Team Development | Growing others, building culture |
| 7 | Systems Thinking | Connecting parts to wholes |
| 8 | Innovation | Pushing boundaries, tolerating ambiguity |
| 9 | Execution & Delivery | Shipping, quality, reliability |

**Scale:** 1–10 (in benchmark data). Not currently user-rated — these are reference profiles only.

### The Gap
There is no mapping between these two frameworks. A user rates themselves on Framework A, gets an archetype from Framework A's model, but then looks at Framework B's radar chart which uses a completely different lens. The two tools don't share data.

---

## 2. Archetype Models (Also Divergent)

### Scorecard Archetypes (6)

These are detected from the user's **craft skill self-ratings**:

```javascript
const ARCHETYPES = [
  {
    name: "The Researcher",
    focus: "Deep in discovery — insight & evidence",
    peaks: ["research", "strategy", "accessibility"]
  },
  {
    name: "The Craftsperson",
    focus: "Pixel-perfect polish, motion & detail",
    peaks: ["visual", "craft", "interaction"]
  },
  {
    name: "The Architect",
    focus: "Systems thinker — scalable foundations",
    peaks: ["systems", "ia", "ops"]
  },
  {
    name: "The Strategist",
    focus: "Big-picture, design meets business",
    peaks: ["strategy", "leadership", "research"]
  },
  {
    name: "The Generalist",
    focus: "Adaptable, cross-functional, fills gaps",
    peaks: []  // No peaks — detected by low variance
  },
  {
    name: "The Champion",
    focus: "Evangelist — teams, culture, buy-in",
    peaks: ["leadership", "ops", "strategy"]
  },
];
```

### Radar Archetypes (7)

These are predefined profiles with **no detection algorithm** — the user manually selects one:

```javascript
const ARCHETYPES = {
  storyteller:  { name: "Experience Storyteller",  sub: "Disney · Pixar · Immersive" },
  systems:      { name: "Systems Architect",       sub: "Enterprise · Fintech · Data" },
  brand:        { name: "Brand Worldbuilder",      sub: "Agencies · Startups · Luxury" },
  interaction:  { name: "Interaction Crafter",     sub: "Product · Design Systems" },
  service:      { name: "Service Designer",        sub: "Gov · Healthcare · Consulting" },
  strategist:   { name: "Research Strategist",     sub: "R&D · Innovation · Policy" },
  technologist: { name: "Creative Technologist",   sub: "Labs · Installations · Experimental" },
};
```

### The Mismatch
| Scorecard | Radar | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| The Researcher | Research Strategist | Closest match |
| The Craftsperson | Interaction Crafter | Partial overlap |
| The Architect | Systems Architect | Close match |
| The Strategist | (no direct match) | Radar has no pure "business strategist" |
| The Generalist | (no equivalent) | Radar doesn't detect blends |
| The Champion | (no equivalent) | Radar has no "evangelist" type |
| (no equivalent) | Experience Storyteller | Scorecard has no narrative/emotion type |
| (no equivalent) | Brand Worldbuilder | Scorecard has no brand identity type |
| (no equivalent) | Service Designer | Scorecard has no service design type |
| (no equivalent) | Creative Technologist | Scorecard has no code-craft hybrid |

---

## 3. Career Level Models (Also Different)

### Scorecard Career Levels (8)

```javascript
const CAREER_LEVELS = [
  { id: "student",   label: "Student / Intern",     yrs: "0 yrs" },
  { id: "junior",    label: "Junior",               yrs: "0–2 yrs" },
  { id: "mid",       label: "Mid-Level",            yrs: "2–5 yrs" },
  { id: "senior",    label: "Senior",               yrs: "5–8 yrs" },
  { id: "lead",      label: "Lead",                 yrs: "7–10 yrs" },
  { id: "principal", label: "Principal / Staff",     yrs: "10+ yrs" },
  { id: "manager",   label: "Manager / Director",   yrs: "8+ yrs" },
  { id: "vp",        label: "VP / Head of Design",  yrs: "12+ yrs" },
];
```

### Radar Career Levels (5)

```javascript
const LEVELS = {
  junior:    "Designer I–II",
  senior:    "Senior Designer",
  staff:     "Staff Designer",
  principal: "Principal / Director",
  fellow:    "Fellow / Sr. Director",
};
```

### The Mismatch
The Scorecard has finer granularity (student, junior, mid, senior, lead, principal, manager, VP) while the Radar collapses these into broader bands. The Scorecard mixes IC and management into one list; the Radar is IC-only (no explicit management track). There is no mapping between the two.

---

## 4. The Benchmark Data Matrix (Radar Only)

350 values: 7 archetypes × 5 levels × 10 competencies. This is the heart of the Radar — each combination produces a unique shape.

The chart remembers its previous state (`prevDataRef`) and morphs from old shape to new shape when selections change.

### Sidebar Radar Geometry (Scorecard — simpler)

```javascript
const c = size / 2;             // center
const r = size / 2 - 28;        // radius with label margin
const levels = 5;               // ring count (matching 1–5 scale)
const step = (Math.PI * 2) / ids.length;

// Grid rings: polygonal (not circular)
const rings = Array.from({ length: levels }, (_, i) => {
  const rr = ((i + 1) / levels) * r;
  return ids.map((_, j) => {
    const a = step * j - Math.PI / 2;
    return `${c + rr * Math.cos(a)},${c + rr * Math.sin(a)}`;
  }).join(" ");
});

// Data shape: straight polygon (no Bezier)
const dataPts = ids.map((id, i) => {
  const a = step * i - Math.PI / 2;
  const d = (scores[id] / 5) * r;  // scale 0–5 → 0–r
  return `${c + d * Math.cos(a)},${c + d * Math.sin(a)}`;
}).join(" ");
```

content.text:
1	# The Designer's Compass — Project Outline
     2	
     3	## The Premise
     4	
     5	Not all designers are the same creature. The field contains fundamentally different species of creative minds, each with their own instincts, strengths, and natural habitats. Understanding *what kind* of designer you are — and what kind you're becoming — is the first step toward a career with intention.
     6	
     7	---
     8	
     9	## I. Designer Archetypes
    10	
    11	Each archetype represents a gravitational pull — where a designer's instincts naturally lead them.
    12	
    13	### A. The Experience Storyteller
    14	- Natural habitat: Disney, Pixar, theme parks, immersive entertainment, game studios
    15	- Core drive: Emotion, narrative arc, delight, wonder
    16	- Portfolio signature: Feels like a journey — you *experience* it, not just view it
    17	- Superpower: Making people feel something specific at a specific moment
    18	
    19	### B. The Systems Architect
    20	- Natural habitat: Enterprise SaaS, fintech, healthcare platforms, data infrastructure
    21	- Core drive: Clarity from complexity, structure, scalability
    22	- Portfolio signature: Before/after transformations of dense information into navigable systems
    23	- Superpower: Making the incomprehensible usable
    24	- **Critical note:** The best systems designers are also storytellers — they translate complexity into narrative for stakeholders and users. Data without story is noise. The ability to explain *why* a system works the way it does, to walk a user through dense information with a guiding hand, is what separates good from great.
    25	
    26	### C. The Brand Worldbuilder
    27	- Natural habitat: Agencies, startups, luxury brands, media companies
    28	- Core drive: Identity, cohesion, cultural resonance
    29	- Portfolio signature: Complete visual universes — logo to packaging to environmental design
    30	- Superpower: Making a brand feel inevitable
    31	
    32	### D. The Interaction Crafter
    33	- Natural habitat: Product studios, design systems teams, platform companies

{"language": "markdown", "code": "# The Designer's Compass — Project Outline

## The Premise

Not all designers are the same creature. The field contains fundamentally different species of creative minds, each with their own instincts, strengths, and natural habitats. Understanding *what kind* of designer you are — and what kind you're becoming — is the first step toward a career with intention.

---

## I. Designer Archetypes

Each archetype represents a gravitational pull — where a designer's instincts naturally lead them.

### A. The Experience Storyteller
- Natural habitat: Disney, Pixar, theme parks, immersive entertainment, game studios
- Core drive: Emotion, narrative arc, delight, wonder
- Portfolio signature: Feels like a journey — you *experience* it, not just view it
- Superpower: Making people feel something specific at a specific moment

### B. The Systems Architect
- Natural habitat: Enterprise SaaS, fintech, healthcare platforms, data infrastructure
- Core drive: Clarity from complexity, structure, scalability
- Portfolio signature: Before/after transformations of dense information into navigable systems
- Superpower: Making the incomprehensible usable
- **Critical note:** The best systems designers are also storytellers — they translate complexity into narrative for stakeholders and users. Data without story is noise. The ability to explain *why* a system works the way it does, to walk a user through dense information with a guiding hand, is what separates good from great.

### C. The Brand Worldbuilder
- Natural habitat: Agencies, startups, luxury brands, media companies
- Core drive: Identity, cohesion, cultural resonance
- Portfolio signature: Complete visual universes — logo to packaging to environmental design
- Superpower: Making a brand feel inevitable

### D. The Interaction Crafter
- Natural habitat: Product studios, design systems teams, platform companies
- Core drive: Micro-moments, motion, precision of response
- Portfolio signature: Prototypes and motion studies that make you say "that feels *right*"
- Superpower: The invisible layer between intent and action

### E. The Service Designer
- Natural habitat: Government, healthcare, consulting, operations-heavy organizations
- Core drive: End-to-end journeys, touchpoint orchestration, systemic change
- Portfolio signature: Journey maps, blueprints, ecosystem diagrams — the big picture
- Superpower: Seeing the whole system, not just the screen

### F. The Research-Led Strategist
- Natural habitat: R&D labs, innovation teams, consulting firms, policy organizations
- Core drive: Evidence, insight, framing the right problem
- Portfolio signature: Insight decks, frameworks, before/after impact narratives
- Superpower: Changing what gets built before a single pixel is placed

### G. The Creative Technologist
- Natural habitat: Labs, studios, installations, experimental product teams
- Core drive: What's possible, merging code and craft
- Portfolio signature: Things that make you ask "how did they do that?"
- Superpower: Bridging the gap between vision and technical reality

---

## II. The Career Ladder

Based on the organizational model provided. Two tracks diverge after the Staff level.

### Entry
| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| UX Intern | Learning the craft in a structured environment |
| UX Apprentice | Guided practice, building foundational skills |

### Individual Contributor Track
| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| UX Designer I | Executing defined problems with guidance |
| UX Designer II | Independently owning design problems |
| Senior UX Designer | Leading complex projects, mentoring others |
| UX Staff Designer | Cross-team influence, setting quality bars |

### The Fork — IC Leadership
| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| UX Principal Designer | Defining design direction for a product area |
| Distinguished Designer | Shaping practice across the organization |
| Design Fellow | Industry-level impact, thought leadership |

### The Fork — People Leadership
| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| UX Manager | Leading a team, developing designers |
| Senior UX Manager | Leading managers, shaping team strategy |
| UX Director | Organizational design strategy |
| Sr. Director | Executive-level design leadership |

### Cross-Track Movement
- Movement between IC and management tracks is possible at every level beyond Staff
- Each transition shifts the competency profile (see Section III)
- The best leaders have walked both paths at some point

---

## III. Competency Radar — The Korn Ferry Layer

Using the Korn Ferry Leadership Architect Global Competency Framework, each role/archetype combination produces a unique radar chart shape.

### Competency Dimensions (adapted for design)
- **Strategic Thinking** — Seeing the big picture, anticipating future needs
- **Business Acumen** — Understanding organizational context and value
- **Stakeholder Influence** — Persuading, aligning, building coalition
- **Craft Mastery** — Depth of design skill and judgment
- **User Empathy & Research** — Understanding people deeply
- **Communication & Storytelling** — Translating ideas across audiences
- **Team Development** — Growing others, building culture
- **Systems Thinking** — Connecting parts to wholes
- **Innovation & Experimentation** — Pushing boundaries, tolerating ambiguity
- **Execution & Delivery** — Shipping, quality, reliability

### How the Shapes Differ
- A **Senior IC Experience Storyteller** peaks on Craft Mastery, User Empathy, Communication
- A **Director-level Systems Architect** peaks on Strategic Thinking, Business Acumen, Systems Thinking
- A **Principal Creative Technologist** peaks on Innovation, Craft Mastery, Execution
- Every archetype shares a need for **Communication & Storytelling** — it's the universal competency

---

## IV. Artifacts to Build

### Artifact 1: The Archetype Explorer
**Type:** Interactive React app
**What it does:**
- Visual cards or an interactive wheel for each designer archetype
- Click into any archetype to see: description, natural habitats, portfolio characteristics, famous examples
- Each archetype has a distinct visual identity / color palette

**Tasks:**
- [ ] Define final archetype list and descriptions
- [ ] Design visual identity per archetype (color, icon, mood)
- [ ] Build interactive card/wheel UI
- [ ] Write detailed content for each archetype

---

### Artifact 2: The Career Ladder Visualization
**Type:** Interactive flowchart / pathway diagram (React)
**What it does:**
- Recreates and enhances the career ladder diagram
- Interactive — click any role to see: level description, typical competencies, what the portfolio looks like at this level
- Shows the IC/Management fork visually with animated transitions
- Crossover paths shown as bridges between the tracks

**Tasks:**
- [ ] Map all roles and relationships from the provided diagram
- [ ] Design the interactive flowchart layout
- [ ] Write role descriptions for each level
- [ ] Build click-to-expand detail panels
- [ ] Animate the fork and crossover paths

---

### Artifact 3: The Competency Radar
**Type:** Interactive radar/rose chart (React + D3 or Recharts)
**What it does:**
- Select an archetype + career level → generates a unique radar chart
- Compare mode: overlay two profiles to see differences
- Animated transitions as you change selections
- Shows which competencies to develop for the next level

**Tasks:**
- [ ] Define competency scores for each archetype × level combination
- [ ] Build the radar chart component with smooth animations
- [ ] Add comparison/overlay mode
- [ ] Design the "growth gap" visualization (current vs. next level)
- [ ] Style with bold, energetic aesthetic per earlier direction

---

### Artifact 4: The Unified Dashboard (Stretch Goal)
**Type:** Single-page app combining all three
**What it does:**
- "Choose your archetype" → see the ladder → see the radar
- A guided flow: *Who am I → Where am I → Where could I go → What do I need to develop*
- The career compass itself

**Tasks:**
- [ ] Design the unified flow and navigation
- [ ] Integrate Artifacts 1–3 into a cohesive experience
- [ ] Add transitions between sections
- [ ] Final polish and storytelling layer

---

## V. Open Questions

1. Should the archetypes be pure types, or should the tool allow blended profiles (e.g., 60% Systems Architect / 40% Storyteller)?
2. How closely should we follow the Korn Ferry framework vs. adapting it for design specifically?
3. Should the career ladder be generic or customizable to specific company structures?
4. Is this a tool for individual designers, or also for managers building teams?

1	# The Skin — Visual Design Language
     2	## Extracted from UX Designer Scorecard + Competency Radar
     3	
     4	Everything in this document is **how it looks and feels**. Nothing about what data exists, how scores are calculated, or what archetypes mean. You could swap this entire skin onto a cooking app and it would still work.
     5	
     6	---
     7	
     8	## 1. Typography Stack
     9	
    10	Three fonts, three jobs. This is the hierarchy that gives both tools their character.
    11	
    12	### Display: Bebas Neue
    13	- **Role:** Headlines, section numbers, big scores, the "wow" moments
    14	- **Where it shows up:** Page title ("UX DESIGNER / SCORECARD"), section numbers (01, 02, 03), composite score in the progress ring, archetype name reveal, stat numbers in the sidebar, footer logo
    15	- **Character:** Tall, compressed, all-caps. Feels athletic, editorial, like a magazine cover. Zero subtlety — it demands attention.
    16	- **Sizes used:** 68px (page title), 32px (archetype name), 26px (card index numbers), 24px (section headers, stat numbers), 22px (score value next to rating dots), 16px (footer logo)
    17	- **Note:** The Radar uses **Syne 700/800** for its title instead — rounder, more geometric, feels more "tech" than "editorial." Decision needed: pick one or keep both for distinct tool personalities.
    18	
    19	### Body: DM Sans
    20	- **Role:** Everything readable — descriptions, labels, buttons, body copy
    21	- **Where it shows up:** Card names, card descriptions, button labels, meta input, subtitle text, archetype descriptions, tooltip text, all paragraph content
    22	- **Weights used:** 300 (subtitle, light labels), 400 (body), 500 (buttons, career level buttons), 600 (check titles, growth item names), 700 (card names, archetype card names)
    23	- **Sizes used:** 14px (card names, meta input), 13px (subtitle, archetype buttons), 12px (career buttons, check titles, skill labels), 11px (card descriptions, archetype focus, bar labels, italic disclaimers), 10px (check sub-text, asset names, compare buttons)
    24	- **Character:** Clean, geometric sans. Reads well at small sizes without feeling clinical.
    25	
    26	### Data/Labels: Space Mono
    27	- **Role:** Anything that feels "system-level" — labels, statuses, metadata, tiny uppercase section dividers
    28	- **Where it shows up:** "WORKSHOP ACTIVITY" tag, "DESIGNER" label, date display, "Novice / Expert" rating labels, all `.rp-` section titles, stat labels, asset statuses, year display in career buttons, status buttons, footer sub-text
    29	- **Sizes used:** 10px (meta label, date), 9px (section titles, rating labels, stat max, growth scores, bar values), 8px (stat labels, status buttons, section titles, career years, footer sub), 7px (smallest stat labels)
    30	- **Character:** Monospace = data. The mechanical precision signals "this is measured, this is a system." The heavy letter-spacing (1–3px) on uppercase labels creates that dashboard instrument feel.
    31	- **Key pattern:** Always uppercase. Always letter-spaced. Never used for readable prose.
    32	
    33	### Font Loading
    34	```html
    35	<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bebas+Neue&family=DM+Sans:wght@300;400;500;600;700&family=Space+Mono:wght@400;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    36	```
    37	Radar additionally loads:
    38	```html
    39	<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Syne:wght@700;800&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    40	```
    41	
    42	---
    43	
    44	## 2. Color System
    45	
    46	### Dark Theme Surfaces (the foundation)
    47	
    48	| Token | Hex | Role |
    49	|-------|-----|------|
    50	| `--bg` | `#08090E` | Deepest background. Page canvas. Almost-black with a blue undertone. |
    51	| `--surface` | `#10121A` | Sidebar background. One step up from void. |
    52	| `--surface2` | `#181B27` | Elevated surfaces: stat cards, career badges, growth items, bar tracks. |
    53	| `--surface3` | `#1E2133` | Card background. The "paper" things sit on. |
    54	| `--border` | `#252836` | Default borders. Barely visible separation lines. |
    55	| `--border-accent` | `#2D3147` | Hover border state. Slightly more visible. |
    56	
    57	The Radar uses slightly different values but the same logic: `#0B0B0F` as its deepest bg.
    58	
    59	**The key insight:** The surface stack is extremely tight — only ~15 lightness points from darkest to lightest. This keeps the dark theme feeling like one continuous space rather than a stack of boxes. Things "emerge from the dark" rather than "sit on layers."
    60	
    61	### Text Opacity Hierarchy
    62	
    63	| Token | Hex | Opacity Equivalent | Role |
    64	|-------|-----|--------------------|------|
    65	| `--text` | `#E8EAF0` | ~92% white | Primary text. Card names, input text, selected states. |
    66	| `--text-dim` | `#6B7194` | ~42% white | Secondary text. Descriptions, labels, unselected items. |
    67	| `--text-muted` | `#3D4162` | ~24% white | Tertiary. Barely-there labels, card index numbers, disabled states. |
    68	
    69	The Radar uses raw `rgba(255,255,255,0.XX)` values instead of tokens — same concept, less organized:
    70	- 0.55 = unselected archetype names
    71	- 0.45 = radar axis labels
    72	- 0.35 = section labels, unselected buttons
    73	- 0.25 = sub-descriptions
    74	- 0.20 = italic disclaimers
    75	- 0.06–0.08 = grid lines, faint borders
    76	
    77	### Accent Colors (per-skill / per-archetype)

1	# The Engine — Portfolio Scorecard
     2	## Workshop Activity: Evaluate Your Portfolio
     3	
     4	---
     5	
     6	## 1. What This Tool Does
     7	
     8	A structured evaluation of a designer's portfolio — both the portfolio site as a container and the individual case studies within it. It works for self-assessment, peer review, or mentor feedback. The reviewer rates dimensions on the same 1–5 dot scale used in the Designer Scorecard.
     9	
    10	The tool produces two layers of output: an overall portfolio health score, and per-case-study quality breakdowns that reveal which projects are carrying the portfolio and which are dragging it down.
    11	
    12	---
    13	
    14	## 2. Relationship to the Designer Scorecard
    15	
    16	**Loosely coupled.** The 7 archetypes from the Competency Radar exist in this tool as context, not dependency:
    17

input.file_text:
# The Designer's Compass

A suite of interactive workshop tools for evaluating UX designers, their portfolios, and career trajectories. Built for the [Jax UX](https://jacksonville-design-community-guide.md) design community.

## Tools

| Tool | Purpose | Status |
|------|---------|--------|
| **Portfolio Scorecard** | Structured portfolio evaluation — shell, case studies, strategy | Active development |
| **UX Designer Scorecard** | Self-assessment of craft skills, soft skills, professional assets | Stable |
| **Competency Radar** | Archetype × career level benchmark radar charts (Korn Ferry–inspired) | Stable |

## Architecture

Each tool follows an **Engine / Skin** separation:

- **Engine** — Data models, scoring algorithms, state shape, archetype detection. Framework-agnostic. Could render as a spreadsheet, CLI, or voice interface.
- **Skin** — Typography, colors, layout, animation, responsive behavior. Pure visual concern.

See [`docs/architecture/`](docs/architecture/) for the full breakdown.

## Tech Stack

Currently built as single-file HTML applications with embedded React (via CDN), CSS, and vanilla JS. The migration plan is:

| Phase | Stack | Notes |
|-------|-------|-------|
| **Current** | Single-file HTML + React CDN + inline CSS/JS | What's in the repo now |
| **Next** | Vite + React + TypeScript | Component extraction, shared models |
| **Future** | Shared design system, Turborepo monorepo | When tool count grows |

## Quick Start

```bash
# No build step needed — just open any HTML file
open src/apps/portfolio-scorecard/index.html

# Or serve locally
npx serve src/apps/
```

## Project Structure

```
designers-compass/
├── docs/
│   ├── architecture/
│   │   ├── the-engine.md           # Engine patterns across all tools
│   │   ├── the-skin.md             # Visual design language
│   │   ├── engine-template.md      # Template for new tool engines
│   │   ├── portfolio-scorecard-engine.md
│   │   └── designer-compass-outline.md
│   └── requirements/
│       ├── portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md
│       └── portfolio-scorecard-requirements.pdf
├── src/
│   ├── apps/
│   │   ├── portfolio-scorecard/
│   │   │   └── index.html          # Main app (single-file)
│   │   ├── ux-designer-scorecard/
│   │   │   └── index.html
│   │   └── competency-radar/
│   │       └── index.html
│   └── shared/                     # Future: extracted shared modules
│       ├── models/                 # Archetypes, career levels, dimensions
│       ├── scoring/                # Score calculation, archetype detection
│       └── styles/                 # Design tokens, shared CSS
├── .claude/
│   └── settings.json               # Claude Code project intelligence
├── CLAUDE.md                        # Claude Code instructions
├── AGENTS.md                        # Multi-agent task coordination
├── .gitignore
├── LICENSE
└── README.md
```

## Requirements Backlog

The Portfolio Scorecard has 16 requirements (REQ-01 through REQ-16) tracked in [`docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md`](docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md). Key items:

- REQ-01–02: Archetype section improvements (explainer text, custom input)
- REQ-03–05: Career stage, company ladder toggle, career goals
- REQ-06: Per-dimension reviewer notes
- REQ-07: Design disciplines multi-select
- REQ-08–10: Branding, export, shareable URLs + QR
- REQ-11–12: Shell dimension updates, general feedback
- REQ-13: Portfolio Purpose Framework (exploratory — always last)
- REQ-14–16: AI fluency, accessibility, naming

## Related Resources

- [Jacksonville Design Community Guide](docs/jacksonville-design-community-guide.md)

## License

MIT — see [LICENSE](LICENSE)

input.file_text:
# CLAUDE.md — Project Intelligence

## What This Project Is

A suite of interactive workshop tools for the Jax UX design community. Three tools exist:

1. **Portfolio Scorecard** — Evaluates a designer's portfolio (shell, case studies, strategy). Active development with 16 REQs in backlog.
2. **UX Designer Scorecard** — Self-assessment of craft skills (10 competencies), soft skills (6), professional assets (10), and experience (10 binary items). Stable.
3. **Competency Radar** — Benchmark radar charts showing expected competency profiles for 7 archetypes × 5 career levels. 350-value data matrix. Stable.

## Architecture: Engine / Skin Separation

Every tool follows a strict separation:

- **Engine** = data models, scoring algorithms, state shape, archetype detection. Lives in `docs/architecture/the-engine.md` and `docs/architecture/portfolio-scorecard-engine.md`. Framework-agnostic — could render as a spreadsheet.
- **Skin** = typography (Bebas Neue / DM Sans / Space Mono), color system (dark theme with tight surface stack), animation, layout. Lives in `docs/architecture/the-skin.md`. Purely visual.

**When modifying code, always identify whether a change is an engine concern or a skin concern.** Do not mix them.

## Current Tech Stack

All three tools are **single-file HTML applications** with:
- React 18 via CDN (`react.production.min.js`, `react-dom.production.min.js`, `babel-standalone`)
- Inline `<style>` blocks with CSS custom properties
- Inline `<script type="text/babel">` blocks with React components
- Google Fonts: Bebas Neue, DM Sans, Space Mono (Radar also uses Syne)
- No build step, no node_modules, no bundler

## Scoring Systems

### Portfolio Scorecard
- 1–5 dot rating scale across 3 sections: Shell (9 dims → 45 pts), Case Studies (variable, 6 dims each → 30 pts per CS), Strategy (4 dims → 20 pts)
- Composite score = weighted sum. Growth areas = bottom 3 dimensions.
- 7 archetypes (manual select, not auto-detected): Storyteller, Systems Architect, Brand Worldbuilder, Interaction Crafter, Service Designer, Research Strategist, Creative Technologist

### UX Designer Scorecard
- Craft skills: 10 competencies rated 1–5 (max 50)
- Soft skills: 6 items rated 1–5 (max 30)
- Professional assets: 10 items with 4 statuses (none/outdated/wip/ready → 0/1/2/3 pts, max 30)
- Experience: 10 binary checkboxes (2 pts each, max 20)
- Grand total: 130 pts
- 6 auto-detected archetypes (Researcher, Craftsperson, Architect, Strategist, Generalist, Champion)

### Competency Radar
- 10 Korn Ferry–adapted competencies rated 1–10 (reference profiles, not user-rated)
- 7 archetypes × 5 career levels = 35 unique profiles
- 350-value benchmark data matrix
- Rose/radar chart with Bezier curve interpolation and animated transitions

## Key Patterns

- **Color accent per archetype/skill**: Each competency and archetype has a dedicated accent color. These are defined as CSS custom properties.
- **Dark theme surface stack**: Very tight lightness range (~15 pts from darkest to lightest). Things "emerge from the dark."
- **Rating dots**: 1–5 scale rendered as a horizontal dot row. Tapping a dot fills it + all previous. Tapping the active dot deselects (toggle off).
- **Pill buttons**: Career levels, archetypes, design disciplines all use pill-style toggle buttons.
- **Monospace labels**: Space Mono, always uppercase, always letter-spaced — signals "system/data."
- **Progress ring**: SVG circle with `stroke-dasharray` animation showing composite percentage.

## Requirements Reference

Portfolio Scorecard has 16 REQs in `docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md`:
- REQ-01 through REQ-12, REQ-14 through REQ-16: Ready to build
- REQ-13: Exploratory — always stays last, requires scorecard version bump first

Each REQ includes: current state, requirement, suggested implementation, state changes, open questions.

## Two Divergent Archetype Systems (Known Tech Debt)

The Scorecard and Radar use **completely different** archetype and career level models. There is no mapping between them. This is documented in `docs/architecture/the-engine.md` §1–3. Reconciliation is a future concern, not a current blocker.

## File Locations

| File | What it is |
|------|-----------|
| `src/apps/portfolio-scorecard/index.html` | Portfolio evaluation tool (active dev) |
| `src/apps/ux-designer-scorecard/index.html` | Designer self-assessment |
| `src/apps/competency-radar/index.html` | Benchmark radar charts |
| `docs/architecture/the-engine.md` | Engine patterns (both tools) |
| `docs/architecture/the-skin.md` | Visual design language |
| `docs/architecture/portfolio-scorecard-engine.md` | Portfolio Scorecard engine spec |
| `docs/architecture/engine-template.md` | Template for new tools |
| `docs/architecture/designer-compass-outline.md` | Project vision & outline |
| `docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md` | REQ backlog |

## Development Workflow

1. Open the HTML file directly in a browser (no build step)
2. Edit inline — all CSS and JS is in the single file
3. Test in browser, iterate
4. When extracting to components (future): move to Vite + React + TypeScript

## Style Guidelines

- Teal (`#00b8a0`) is the primary accent for Portfolio Scorecard
- Coral (`#e8365a`) is the primary accent for UX Designer Scorecard  
- Dark theme is the default; light theme exists for Portfolio Scorecard
- Bebas Neue for display/headlines, DM Sans for body, Space Mono for data labels
- Never mix engine logic into CSS/layout concerns

- 7 archetypes (manual select, not auto-detected): Storyteller, Systems Architect, Brand Worldbuilder, Interaction Crafter, Service Designer, Research Strategist, Creative Technologist

### UX Designer Scorecard
- Craft skills: 10 competencies rated 1–5 (max 50)
- Soft skills: 6 items rated 1–5 (max 30)
- Professional assets: 10 items with 4 statuses (none/outdated/wip/ready → 0/1/2/3 pts, max 30)
- Experience: 10 binary checkboxes (2 pts each, max 20)
- Grand total: 130 pts
- 6 auto-detected archetypes (Researcher, Craftsperson, Architect, Strategist, Generalist, Champion)

### Competency Radar
- 10 Korn Ferry–adapted competencies rated 1–10 (reference profiles, not user-rated)
- 7 archetypes × 5 career levels = 35 unique profiles
- 350-value benchmark data matrix
- Rose/radar chart with Bezier curve interpolation and animated transitions

## Key Patterns

- **Color accent per archetype/skill**: Each competency and archetype has a dedicated accent color. These are defined as CSS custom properties.
- **Dark theme surface stack**: Very tight lightness range (~15 pts from darkest to lightest). Things "emerge from the dark."
- **Rating dots**: 1–5 scale rendered as a horizontal dot row. Tapping a dot fills it + all previous. Tapping the active dot deselects (toggle off).
- **Pill buttons**: Career levels, archetypes, design disciplines all use pill-style toggle buttons.
- **Monospace labels**: Space Mono, always uppercase, always letter-spaced — signals "system/data."
- **Progress ring**: SVG circle with `stroke-dasharray` animation showing composite percentage.

## Requirements Reference

Portfolio Scorecard has 16 REQs in `docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md`:
- REQ-01 through REQ-12, REQ-14 through REQ-16: Ready to build
- REQ-13: Exploratory — always stays last, requires scorecard version bump first

Each REQ includes: current state, requirement, suggested implementation, state changes, open questions.

## Two Divergent Archetype Systems (Known Tech Debt)

The Scorecard and Radar use **completely different** archetype and career level models. There is no mapping between them. This is documented in `docs/architecture/the-engine.md` §1–3. Reconciliation is a future concern, not a current blocker.

## File Locations

| File | What it is |
|------|-----------|
| `src/apps/portfolio-scorecard/index.html` | Portfolio evaluation tool (active dev) |
| `src/apps/ux-designer-scorecard/index.html` | Designer self-assessment |
| `src/apps/competency-radar/index.html` | Benchmark radar charts |

| `docs/architecture/designer-compass-outline.md` | Project vision & outline |
| `docs/requirements/portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md` | REQ backlog |

## Development Workflow

1. Open the HTML file directly in a browser (no build step)
2. Edit inline — all CSS and JS is in the single file
3. Test in browser, iterate
4. When extracting to components (future): move to Vite + React + TypeScript

## Style Guidelines

- Teal (`#00b8a0`) is the primary accent for Portfolio Scorecard
- Coral (`#e8365a`) is the primary accent for UX Designer Scorecard  
- Dark theme is the default; light theme exists for Portfolio Scorecard
- Bebas Neue for display/headlines, DM Sans for body, Space Mono for data labels
- Never mix engine logic into CSS/layout concerns

# AGENTS.md — Multi-Agent Task Coordination

## Agent Roles

### Engine Agent
**Scope:** Data models, scoring algorithms, state shape, archetype detection, dimension definitions.
**Files:** `docs/architecture/`, `src/shared/models/`, `src/shared/scoring/`
**Rules:**
- Never touch CSS, layout, animation, or visual properties
- All changes must be framework-agnostic (no React-specific code in engine logic)
- Every scoring change must update the corresponding engine doc
- New dimensions require: id, name, desc, anchor_low, anchor_high, scale, max score

### Skin Agent
**Scope:** Typography, colors, spacing, animation, responsive behavior, print styles.
**Files:** `docs/architecture/the-skin.md`, `src/shared/styles/`, CSS blocks in app HTML files
**Rules:**
- Never modify scoring logic, data models, or state shape
- All color values must use CSS custom properties (never hardcoded hex in components)
- Typography follows the 3-font hierarchy: Bebas Neue (display), DM Sans (body), Space Mono (data)
- Surface stack must stay tight (~15 lightness pts from darkest to lightest)

### Feature Agent
**Scope:** Implementing REQs from the requirements backlog.
**Files:** `src/apps/portfolio-scorecard/`, `docs/requirements/`
**Rules:**
- Read the full REQ spec before starting (includes current state, requirement, state changes, open questions)
- Identify whether each part of the REQ is an engine change or skin change
- Update the requirements doc with implementation notes after completing a REQ
- REQ-13 is always last — never implement before other REQs are complete

### QA Agent
**Scope:** Testing, accessibility, cross-browser validation.
**Rules:**
- Verify scoring math against engine docs
- Check all interactive states (hover, active, disabled, selected)
- Validate keyboard navigation and screen reader behavior
- Test both light and dark themes
- Verify print stylesheet output

## Coordination Rules

1. **One REQ at a time.** Don't batch unrelated requirements.
2. **Engine before skin.** If a feature needs both, implement the engine change first, verify scoring, then add the visual layer.
3. **Always check the engine doc** before assuming how scoring works. The doc is the source of truth.
4. **State changes are breaking.** Any modification to the state shape (new fields, renamed fields, type changes) must be called out explicitly and documented.
5. **No orphaned UI.** Every input must connect to state. Every state value must surface in the results panel.

## Search Results: Nothing Exists Like This

I searched extensively across GitHub topics, open-source repos, and the web. Everything falls into three irrelevant buckets:

1. **Financial portfolio tools** — investment analysis, backtesting engines
2. **Developer portfolio *website* templates** — React templates for showing *your own* work
3. **Generic radar chart libraries** — react-radar-chart, recharts, d3 wrappers

Your project is a **portfolio evaluation framework** — a tool for *reviewing and scoring* someone else's design portfolio in a structured workshop setting. That concept doesn't exist as an open-source project anywhere. GitLab has a documented UX Scorecard *process*, but it's a handbook page, not a tool. You're building the tool. No wheel to reinvent here.

## What I've Built So Far

I created the repo skeleton at `/home/claude/designers-compass/` with:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `README.md` | Project overview, tool descriptions, structure, quick start |
| `CLAUDE.md` | **Project intelligence file** — architecture, scoring systems, patterns, file map, style rules. This is what Claude Code reads first. |
| `AGENTS.md` | Multi-agent coordination — Engine Agent, Skin Agent, Feature Agent, QA Agent roles and rules |
| `.claude/settings.json` | Claude Code permissions |
| `.gitignore` | Standard web project ignores |
| `LICENSE` | MIT |
| Directory structure | `docs/architecture/`, `docs/requirements/`, `src/apps/{3 tools}/`, `src/shared/` |

## What Still Needs to Happen

To finish the export, these remaining steps are needed:

1. **Copy the 3 HTML apps** into `src/apps/*/index.html`
2. **Copy the architecture docs** (the-engine.md, the-skin.md, engine-template.md, portfolio-scorecard-engine.md, designer-compass-outline.md) into `docs/architecture/`

content.text:
Good news — tools do exist for this. Here's what's out there:

## Existing Tools for Exporting Claude Projects

There are **three layers** to export and several tools that handle each:

### 1. Project Knowledge Files (your uploaded docs)

**[Claude Project Files Extractor](https://greasyfork.org/en/scripts/541467-claude-project-files-extractor)** — A Tampermonkey userscript that downloads all your project knowledge files as a single ZIP. Handles PDFs (actual files, not text extraction), markdown, CSVs, and everything else. One-click, runs in-browser. This is probably your best bet for the knowledge files themselves.

**[withLinda/claude-project-knowledge-exporter](https://github.com/withLinda/claude-project-knowledge-exporter)** — A bookmarklet (or console script) that exports project knowledge as Markdown + JSON. Three modes: consolidated, individual files, or complete. Note: bookmarklets may be blocked by CSP now, so the console paste method is more reliable.

### 2. Project Conversations

**[withLinda/claude-project-conversations-exporter](https://github.com/withLinda/claude-project-conversations-exporter)** — Exports ALL conversations from a Claude Project as individual Markdown files with an index. Handles tool calls, attachments, and metadata.

**[agoramachina/claude-exporter](https://github.com/agoramachina/claude-exporter)** — Chrome extension with bulk export, branch-aware conversation export, artifact extraction as separate files, and ZIP output. Supports JSON, Markdown, and Plain Text.

### 3. Claude.ai Project → Claude Code Migration (the full workflow)

**[This Zenn guide](https://zenn.dev/sora_biz/articles/claude-code-migration-guide?locale=en)** is the closest thing to a documented end-to-end pattern. The workflow it describes:

1. Export your data from Claude.ai (Settings → Privacy → Export Data)
2. Place the exported JSON files in your project directory
3. Have Claude Code convert your accumulated knowledge into `CLAUDE.md` and `.claude/rules/*.md` files
4. Those files auto-load every time Claude Code launches in that directory

### What Doesn't Exist Yet

There's **no single tool** that does the full pipeline: "take a Claude.ai Project → produce a ready-to-go GitHub repo with CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, proper directory structure, and all files organized." That's the gap. Every existing tool handles one piece (download files, export conversations, migrate memories), but the *assembly* into a Claude Code–ready repo structure is still manual.

So the repo skeleton I built for you is still the right move — it's just that you can use these tools to pull your files out of Claude.ai first, then drop them into the structure. Want me to finish populating it with your project files now?

So the actual source code is just the three HTML files, while everything else is documentation, specifications, and design systems supporting them.

For Claude Code to work effectively, I need a **CLAUDE.md** file that acts as the project's central intelligence—something that immediately orients Claude to the codebase structure, the design system, and how all these pieces fit together. This should be the first thing Claude reads to understand what it's working with.

The real question is whether to give Claude Code the full conversation transcripts or a distilled handoff document. Transcripts have all the nuance and decision-making context, but they're verbose and burn through the context window. A handoff doc is lean and focused—capturing decisions made, the reasoning behind them, current state, and what needs to happen next—but risks losing important details. I'm leaning toward a handoff doc as the primary reference, with transcripts available if Claude Code needs to dig deeper into specific decisions.

For organizing the files, the natural groupings are becoming clearer: the actual deliverables (the HTML apps), the architectural specs that explain how everything works, and the requirements that define what needs to be built. I'm exploring a second structure organized by product instead—each tool gets its own folder with its specs and requirements bundled together, while shared patterns like the engine and skin live in a common directory.

Now I'm considering a third approach that flattens everything into a single root level with descriptive filenames, which would make it easier to see all assets at once but might get cluttered as the project grows. The real question is whether I want to optimize for discoverability or for logical grouping.

For Claude Code specifically, I'm realizing that CLAUDE.md gets loaded automatically from the root, and I can set up rules in .claude/rules/ that also load automatically. Everything else needs to be explicitly referenced or searched. So the structure should really be about what makes sense for Claude Code to work with—a well-organized CLAUDE.md paired with clearly named files beats raw conversation transcripts, since transcripts are noisy and full of dead ends that would waste the context window. I should save transcripts as reference material that Claude Code can pull from when needed, but not make them the primary handoff mechanism.
