content.text:
OK, here's my analysis. I looked at each file by what it *does*, not what it's *about*.

---

## What each file actually is

Every file in this project serves one of four jobs:

**THE PRODUCT** (things that run, things Claude Code edits):
| File | Size | Status |
|------|------|--------|
| `portfolio-scorecard.html` | 938 lines, 49K | Active development |
| `ux-designer-scorecard.html` | 858 lines, 45K | Stable |
| `competency-radar.html` | 607 lines, 24K | Stable |

**THE SPECS** (code-heavy reference — HOW things work):
| File | Code Blocks | What it is |
|------|-------------|------------|
| `the-engine.md` | 40 | Cross-tool data models, scoring, archetypes |
| `the-skin.md` | 26 | Cross-tool visual language, tokens, typography |
| `portfolio-scorecard-engine.md` | 34 | Portfolio scorecard–specific engine |
| `engine-template.md` | 14 | Blank template for creating new tools |

**THE BACKLOG** (what to build next):
| File | Code Blocks | What it is |
|------|-------------|------------|
| `portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md` | 70 | 16 REQs with state changes, open questions |
| `portfolioscorecardrequirements.pdf` | — | Same content as PDF (300K, duplicate) |

**THE VISION** (prose, zero code — context and direction):
| File | Size | What it is |
|------|------|------------|
| `designer-compass-outline.md` | 9K | Project vision, archetypes, artifacts to build |
| `jacksonville-design-community-guide.md` | 69K | Community strategy (large, loosely connected) |

---

## My proposed structure

```
designers-compass/
│
├── CLAUDE.md                     # Orientation — auto-loaded by Claude Code
├── README.md                     # Human-facing project overview
├── designer-compass-outline.md   # Project vision (root = important)
│
├── src/                          # What Claude Code EDITS
│   ├── portfolio-scorecard.html
│   ├── ux-designer-scorecard.html
│   └── competency-radar.html
│
├── specs/                        # What Claude Code READS to understand the system
│   ├── the-engine.md
│   ├── the-skin.md
│   ├── portfolio-scorecard-engine.md
│   └── engine-template.md
│
├── backlog/                      # What Claude Code READS to know what to build
│   ├── portfolio-scorecard-requirements.md
│   └── portfolio-scorecard-requirements.pdf
│
├── reference/                    # Background material — read once, consult rarely
│   └── jacksonville-design-community-guide.md
│
├── transcripts/                  # Conversation history — archived, searchable
│   └── .gitkeep
│
├── .gitignore
└── LICENSE
```

**Why this structure:**

The folder names describe the *action* Claude Code takes with the contents — edit, read-to-understand, read-to-plan, or search-for-history. The vision doc sits at root because it's short (9K) and important enough to be visible alongside CLAUDE.md. The community guide goes in `reference/` because it's 69K and tangential — Claude Code shouldn't load it unless explicitly asked about the Jax UX community.

---

## Question 2: What additional files does Claude Code need?

There's a spectrum between "just give it the transcripts" and "write a whole handoff doc":

**Transcripts alone won't work well.** They're noisy, full of dead ends, and eat context window. A single long conversation could be 50-100K tokens — that's half of Claude Code's working memory gone before it's written a line of code.

**But the specs + requirements already cover 90% of what a handoff doc would say.** Your project is unusually well-documented. The engine docs describe HOW, the requirements describe WHAT, and the vision doc describes WHY. Most projects don't have this.

**The gap is DECISIONS.** The specs say what the system does. They don't say "we considered X but rejected it because Y." That context lives in your conversations and it's valuable when Claude Code hits an ambiguous situation.

Here's what I'd add:

| File | Purpose | Auto-loaded? |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Orientation: what this project is, where things are, how to work on it, current state | Yes — auto-loaded every session |
| `DECISIONS.md` | Key decisions log: what was considered, what was chosen, why. Distilled from conversations, not a transcript dump. | No — Claude Code reads when it needs context on a specific decision |
| `.claude/rules/engine-skin-separation.md` | Rule: always identify if a change is engine or skin. Never mix them. | Yes — auto-loaded as a rule |
| `.claude/rules/scoring-patterns.md` | Rule: 1-5 dot scale patterns, how to add new dimensions, state shape conventions | Yes — auto-loaded as a rule |

**The CLAUDE.md IS the handoff doc.** Don't create a separate one — that's just duplication. But keep it tight (under 3K tokens) so it loads fast and leaves room for actual work.

**DECISIONS.md is the missing piece.** I can generate a first draft from this conversation and the project files. It's the bridge between "here's what we built" and "here's why we built it this way."

---

## Question 3: Saving transcripts

Three approaches, ranked by completeness:

**Best for your case: [agoramachina/claude-exporter](https://github.com/agoramachina/claude-exporter)** — Chrome extension. Exports conversations as Markdown, JSON, or plain text. Handles artifacts as separate files. Bulk export as ZIP. Branch-aware. This is the most full-featured option and it can filter by project.

**Runner-up: [withLinda's project conversation exporter](https://github.com/withLinda/claude-project-conversations-exporter)** — Console script specifically for project conversations. Generates an `index.md` with links to individual conversation files. Good if you want project-scoped export without installing an extension.

**For the knowledge files specifically: [Claude Project Files Extractor](https://greasyfork.org/en/scripts/541467-claude-project-files-extractor)** — Tampermonkey script that downloads all uploaded project knowledge files as a ZIP. You'd want this alongside the conversation exporter to get everything.

**For transcripts in the repo, I'd recommend:**

- Export as Markdown (most readable, grep-able)
- Store in `transcripts/` with date-prefixed filenames: `2026-03-16_portfolio-scorecard-archetype-rework.md`
- Add `transcripts/` to `.gitignore` initially — these are large and noisy. Keep them local. If you want them version-controlled, use a separate branch or git-lfs.
- The DECISIONS.md is what gets committed instead — the distilled version.

---

Want me to see your structure idea now, or should I go ahead and build out the CLAUDE.md and DECISIONS.md drafts based on everything in this project?

content.text:
Here's what I found:

## Safety Assessment: agoramachina/claude-exporter

**The permissions are narrow and reasonable.** From the Firefox extension listing, which documents them explicitly:

- **`activeTab`** — interact with the current Claude.ai page (standard for any page-reading extension)
- **`storage`** — save your Organization ID and settings locally
- **`tabs`** — detect conversation IDs from the URL
- **`https://claude.ai/*`** — fetch conversation data from Claude's own API

That's it. No `<all_urls>`, no access to other sites, no `webRequest` interception. It's scoped exclusively to claude.ai.

**Other good signs:**
- All conversation data is processed locally in the browser — nothing is sent to external servers
- User-provided data is sanitized using an escapeHtml() helper function before being inserted into HTML
- The JSZip library is a well-established open-source package, and the Firefox review team flagged its `Function` constructor usage — the developer documented it transparently
- It's forked from socketteer/Claude-Conversation-Exporter, which is the more established original (the fork adds artifact export and Firefox support)
- 270 commits, source is fully readable, MIT licensed
- Not on the Chrome Web Store (sideloaded from source), which actually means you can audit every line before installing

**The one caveat:** it authenticates using your existing Claude.ai session cookie — it makes API calls to Claude's backend as *you*. That's how it works, and it's the same thing you do when you click around claude.ai manually. But it means if the extension code were ever compromised in a future update, it could theoretically read all your conversations. That risk exists with any extension that has host permissions on a site you're logged into.

**My take:** it's safe to use. The permissions are minimal, the code is open and auditable, processing is local, and the scope is locked to claude.ai. If you want to be extra cautious, install from source rather than an extension store so you control exactly which version you're running.

content.thinking: Clean. Every single network call goes exclusively to claude.ai. No external URLs, no eval, no dynamic code execution, no data exfiltration vectors. The sendMessage calls are all internal Chrome extension messaging between popup/browse/content scripts.

summaries.summary: Verifying network security and internal messaging integrity.
Verified security architecture and internal messaging protocols.

content.text:
It's clean. Here's the full picture:

**Every single network call goes to `claude.ai` and nowhere else.** Six `fetch()` calls total across the whole codebase, all hitting the same API:

- `https://claude.ai/api/organizations/{orgId}/chat_conversations` — list your conversations
- `https://claude.ai/api/organizations/{orgId}/chat_conversations/{id}?tree=True...` — get a single conversation
- `https://claude.ai/api/organizations/{orgId}/projects` — list projects

That's it. No other domains. No `XMLHttpRequest`, no `WebSocket`, no `eval()`, no `new Function()`. The `sendMessage` calls are all internal Chrome extension messaging between the popup, browse page, and content script — never leaving your browser.

**Permissions confirmed minimal:**
- `activeTab`, `storage`, `tabs`, `scripting`
- Host access locked to `https://claude.ai/*` only
- Firefox manifest explicitly declares `"data_collection_permissions": {"required": ["none"]}`

No data goes anywhere except from Claude's servers to your downloads folder. You're good.

summaries.summary: Investigated transcript preservation tools and extension alternatives.

content.text:
Here's the landscape:

## Notion

There are several options, but they vary in trustworthiness:

**[ClaudeAI to Notion](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/claudeai-to-notion/lmhbkobhomjjikgkdfpjkcmpafikopbe)** (by chatgpt2notion.com) — The most full-featured option. Batch sync, skip/overwrite/duplicate collision handling, preserves code blocks and formatting. All processing is local in your browser. The extension talks directly to Notion via OAuth — no third-party servers in the middle, no data exhaust. That said, this is from the same developer who makes the ChatGPT-to-Notion suite, so it's a commercial product — worth checking their privacy policy closely.

**[Chat to Notion](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/chat-to-notion/oojndninaelbpllebamcojkdecjjhcle)** (by L-a-r-t) — Open source, 4.6 stars, 107 ratings. Supports ChatGPT, Deepseek, Claude, and Mistral. The developer is transparent about one tradeoff: some data such as your Notion user id and the key that allows communication with your Notion workspace are stored remotely instead of locally where they could be easily compromised. The code for this is open source. So it does have a server component, but the code is on GitHub for auditing.

**[AINote to Notion](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/ainote-to-notion/oilkglbeeameahfbpbcckmlaicmgbihc)** — Simpler, one-click save. Personal development plugin, not commercial. 4.7 stars but fewer reviews. Less feature-rich.

## Linear

No Chrome extension exists for saving Claude transcripts to Linear. Linear has a Claude MCP connector — but that goes the other direction (Claude reading/writing Linear issues from within a conversation, not archiving conversations into Linear). If you wanted transcripts in Linear, your best bet would be exporting to Markdown with the agoramachina exporter, then using Linear's API or a simple script to create issues/docs from those files.

## Other notable extensions for saving transcripts

**[AI Chat Exporter](https://www.ai-chat-exporter.net/en/welcome)** — Chrome Web Store extension. Exports to PDF, Markdown, Text, JSON, CSV, and Image. Lets you select specific responses rather than the full conversation. All export formats other than PDF are processed entirely within your browser. PDF is temporarily processed on their servers and immediately deleted. Users seem to love it for long conversations.

**[Chat Memo](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/chat-memo-auto-save-ai-ch/memnnheiikbfdcobfkghhfihnegkfici)** — 4.7 stars, Featured. Auto-saves conversations from ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and more as you chat. Card-style browsing, full-text search, one-click export. Good if you want a "never think about it" background backup running.

**[Claude Toolbox](https://www.ai-toolkit.site/)** — Adds search across all conversations, bookmarking individual messages, and export. The search feature is the differentiator — useful if your main pain is *finding* things in your history.

## My recommendation for your workflow

Since you're trying to get transcripts into a GitHub repo's `transcripts/` folder, the cleanest path is still **agoramachina/claude-exporter** → export as Markdown → drop into the repo. The Notion extensions are great if Notion is your knowledge base, but they add a middleman between you and the files. For your use case — preserving context for Claude Code — local Markdown files are the most direct format.

content.text: why export as markdown?

content.thinking: The user is questioning my assumption that Markdown is the best export format. Let me think about what actually matters for their use case — feeding transcripts to Claude Code for context.

summaries.summary: Thinking about the rationale and benefits of markdown export format.
Reconsidered format choice for transcript context delivery.

content.text:
Fair question. I defaulted to Markdown without justifying it. Let me think about what you're actually using these for.

The transcripts go into a `transcripts/` folder that Claude Code reads for context. So the real question is: what format does Claude Code consume most effectively?

**JSON** is actually the better choice. It preserves the full structure — who said what, timestamps, which model was used, conversation branches, artifacts as separate objects. Claude Code can parse it programmatically and extract exactly what it needs. Markdown flattens all of that into a readable document, which loses metadata.

**JSON also keeps your options open.** You can always convert JSON → Markdown later with a script. You can't go the other direction without losing data.

The only argument for Markdown is human readability — if *you* want to open a transcript and skim it. But that's what the browse/search features in the extension are for, or what Claude Code itself can do when you ask it to summarize a conversation.

Export as JSON. Store the raw data. Let Claude Code interpret it.
